PHASE 3 - FUSION-NATIVE ECOSYSTEM
Good. Phase 3 is where Fusion stops borrowing gravity and creates its own.
This phase does three irreversible things:
1. Defines Fusion-native extensions (not VS Code shaped)
2. Introduces an MCP-first SDK with capability contracts
3. Makes trust + capability the execution boundary, not code location
After this, Fusion is no longer downstream of anyone.

Core Reframe
VS Code extensions are:
editor plugins that happen to expose commands
Fusion extensions are:
capability providers that happen to expose tools
That difference is everything.

New Crates
crates/
+-- fusion-capability-spec/
+-- fusion-extension-sdk/
+-- fusion-extension-runtime/
+-- fusion-extension-registry/
+-- fusion-extension-tests/

fusion-capability-spec - Capability-First ABI
This is the new contract.
Tools exist only if capabilities are granted.
crates/fusion-capability-spec/Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "fusion-capability-spec"
version = "1.0.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
src/lib.rs
use serde::{Serialize, Deserialize};

#[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize, Clone)]
pub struct Capability {
    pub name: String,
    pub description: String,
    pub risk_level: RiskLevel,
}

#[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize, Clone)]
pub enum RiskLevel {
    Low,
    Medium,
    High,
    Critical,
}
This becomes machine-readable trust input later.

fusion-extension-sdk - MCP-First Tool SDK
This is what developers build against.
crates/fusion-extension-sdk/Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "fusion-extension-sdk"
version = "1.0.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
fusion-capability-spec = { path = "../fusion-capability-spec" }
serde_json = "1.0"
src/lib.rs
use fusion_capability_spec::Capability;

pub trait FusionExtension {
    fn id(&self) -> &'static str;
    fn capabilities(&self) -> Vec<Capability>;
    fn tools(&self) -> Vec<FusionTool>;
}

pub struct FusionTool {
    pub name: String,
    pub handler: fn(serde_json::Value) -> serde_json::Value,
}
No globals.
No editor state.
No hidden APIs.

fusion-extension-runtime - Capability-Gated Execution
This runtime replaces implicit trust with explicit contracts.
crates/fusion-extension-runtime/Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "fusion-extension-runtime"
version = "1.0.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
fusion-extension-sdk = { path = "../fusion-extension-sdk" }
fusion-capability-spec = { path = "../fusion-capability-spec" }
fusion-policy = { path = "../fusion-policy" }
src/lib.rs
use fusion_extension_sdk::{FusionExtension, FusionTool};
use fusion_policy::{Policy, PolicyDecision};

pub struct ExtensionRuntime<P: Policy> {
    policy: P,
}

impl<P: Policy> ExtensionRuntime<P> {
    pub fn new(policy: P) -> Self {
        Self { policy }
    }

    pub fn execute(
        &self,
        ext: &dyn FusionExtension,
        tool_name: &str,
        input: serde_json::Value,
    ) -> Result<serde_json::Value, String> {

        for cap in ext.capabilities() {
            match self.policy.evaluate(&cap.name) {
                PolicyDecision::Deny(r) => return Err(r),
                PolicyDecision::Allow => {}
            }
        }

        let tool = ext.tools()
            .into_iter()
            .find(|t| t.name == tool_name)
            .ok_or("Tool not found")?;

        Ok((tool.handler)(input))
    }
}
Execution now requires:
? Capability approval
? Policy clearance
? Explicit tool selection

fusion-extension-registry - No Editor Assumptions
This replaces "marketplaces" with capability catalogs.
crates/fusion-extension-registry/Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "fusion-extension-registry"
version = "1.0.0"
edition = "2021"
src/lib.rs
use std::collections::HashMap;

pub struct ExtensionRegistry {
    extensions: HashMap<String, String>, // id ? checksum
}

impl ExtensionRegistry {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self { extensions: HashMap::new() }
    }

    pub fn register(&mut self, id: &str, checksum: &str) {
        self.extensions.insert(id.into(), checksum.into());
    }

    pub fn verify(&self, id: &str, checksum: &str) -> bool {
        self.extensions.get(id).map(|c| c == checksum).unwrap_or(false)
    }
}
This feeds directly into Phase 4 trust scoring.

Extension Test (End-to-End)
fusion-extension-tests/src/lib.rs
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use fusion_extension_sdk::*;
    use fusion_capability_spec::*;
    use fusion_policy::AllowListPolicy;
    use fusion_extension_runtime::ExtensionRuntime;

    struct TestExt;

    impl FusionExtension for TestExt {
        fn id(&self) -> &'static str { "test.ext" }

        fn capabilities(&self) -> Vec<Capability> {
            vec![Capability {
                name: "compute.basic".into(),
                description: "Basic compute".into(),
                risk_level: RiskLevel::Low,
            }]
        }

        fn tools(&self) -> Vec<FusionTool> {
            vec![FusionTool {
                name: "echo".into(),
                handler: |v| v,
            }]
        }
    }

    #[test]
    fn extension_executes_with_capability() {
        let policy = AllowListPolicy::new(vec!["compute.basic".into()]);
        let runtime = ExtensionRuntime::new(policy);

        let ext = TestExt;
        let result = runtime.execute(&ext, "echo", serde_json::json!({"x":1}));
        assert!(result.is_ok());
    }
}

What Phase 3 Locks In
Fusion now has:
Native extension format
MCP-first SDK
Capability-based execution
Editor-free runtime
Registry & provenance hooks
Clean trust boundary
At this point:
VS Code extensions are inputs
Fusion extensions are infrastructure
This is exactly how Docker escaped being "just a CLI".
